ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'fried fish food product' class; no specific entry for kembong (Indian mackerel) deep-fried preparation exists. Closest defensible parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers deep-fried fish generically; no species-specific code for Rastrelliger kanagurta (Indian mackerel / kembong) exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for fried kembong fish as a prepared food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one whole piece of deep-fried kembong fish, approximately 120 g, as typically served as a side dish alongside rice in a Singaporean or Malaysian meal.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Fish, mackerel, Indian, cooked (NDB 15046), adapted for deep-fried preparation with salt and turmeric seasoning; cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient composition data for fried fish
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient anchor: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked Indian mackerel (NDB 15046), adapted to reflect deep-frying in palm oil with salt and turmeric seasoning. The input record's per-100g values (energy 291.67 kcal, protein 20.83 g, fat 20.83 g, carbohydrate 4.17 g) are consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for cooked mackerel adjusted for fat uptake from deep-frying. Micronutrient values (calcium 41.67 mg, iron 1.25 mg, cholesterol 58.33 mg, saturated fat 8.33 g, trans fat 0.17 g) are retained from the input and are plausible against USDA SR Legacy cooked mackerel data. HPB Singapore Foodo (https://foodo.sg/) was consulted as a secondary cross-reference for Singaporean fried fish preparations. Potassium estimated at 350 mg/100 g based on USDA SR Legacy data for cooked mackerel. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat data not available for this specific deep-fried preparation; set to -1.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for deep-fried kembong fish. GI is estimated at 40 (low) based on the analogous category of battered or coated fried fish products reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of GI Values (Atkinson et al., 2008). The carbohydrate content of 4.17 g/100 g is primarily from the light turmeric-salt coating. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 5.0 g) / 100 = 2.0, where carbohydrate per 120 g serving = 4.17 × 1.2 = 5.0 g. GL of 2.0 is low. This analogous estimate is conservative and clinically appropriate; the actual GI may be lower given the minimal coating.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 375 mg per 100 g; one 120 g serving delivers approximately 450 mg sodium, representing about 20% of the 2,000 mg daily limit recommended by HPB Singapore.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one small piece and request reduced-salt preparation; pairing with potassium-rich vegetables can help offset the sodium load from this dish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (4.17 g/100 g) and zero sugar support glycaemic stability, but high total fat (20.83 g/100 g) and saturated fat (8.33 g/100 g) from deep-frying may impair long-term insulin sensitivity.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fried kembong fish is acceptable for people with Type 2 Diabetes in small portions due to its negligible carbohydrate content and low glycemic load (GL 2.0 per serving), but the high saturated fat from deep-frying should be moderated; grilled or steamed preparation is strongly preferred for regular consumption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 8.33 g/100 g and trans fat at 0.17 g/100 g from deep-frying in palm oil raise LDL cholesterol risk; however, kembong fish is naturally rich in omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) which are cardioprotective.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, grilled or steamed kembong fish is strongly preferred over deep-fried preparation to preserve the cardioprotective omega-3 benefit while eliminating the saturated and trans fat burden introduced by deep-frying in palm oil.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and the palm oil contributes saturated fat at 8.33 g/100 g, both of which promote systemic inflammation and worsen joint symptoms.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-fried preparation is a disqualifier for rheumatoid arthritis dietary management; request grilled or steamed kembong fish with turmeric to retain the anti-inflammatory curcumin and omega-3 benefits without the pro-inflammatory AGE and saturated fat burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Kembong Fish (Side Dish) is a popular Singaporean and Malaysian side dish consisting of whole or halved kembong fish (Indian mackerel, Rastrelliger kanagurta) that has been seasoned with salt and ground turmeric, then deep-fried in palm oil until the skin is crispy and golden brown while the flesh remains moist and flavourful. Kembong is a small, oily, silver-blue fish widely available in wet markets and hawker centres across Singapore and Malaysia, prized for its rich umami flavour, firm white flesh, and high omega-3 fatty acid content. The turmeric imparts a characteristic golden colour and mild earthy aroma, while the salt seasoning penetrates the flesh during marination. Deep-frying at high temperature creates a crispy exterior with a Maillard-browned crust. Nutritionally, per 100 g this dish provides approximately 292 kcal, 20.8 g protein, 20.8 g total fat (8.3 g saturated), 4.2 g carbohydrate, and 375 mg sodium. The high protein content makes it a valuable source of complete amino acids. The omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) naturally present in kembong are partially retained after frying and offer cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory benefits. However, the deep-frying process introduces significant saturated fat and a small amount of trans fat (0.17 g/100 g) from palm oil, which raises concern for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis management. The sodium content of 375 mg/100 g requires moderation for individuals with hypertension. A standard serving is one piece (approximately 120 g), delivering around 450 mg sodium and 25 g protein. The glycemic load per serving is low at 2.0, making it suitable for glycaemic management in small portions. This dish is halal-certified and gluten-free, making it suitable for a wide range of dietary requirements. It is commonly served alongside steamed white rice, sambal, and vegetables as part of a traditional Singaporean or Malaysian meal.
common_user_queries: How many calories in fried kembong fish?
Is fried Indian mackerel good for high blood pressure?
Fried fish side dish calories Singapore

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are anchored to USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked Indian mackerel (NDB 15046), adapted for deep-frying in palm oil with salt and turmeric seasoning, and cross-referenced against HPB Singapore Foodo data for fried fish preparations. The input record's provenance is listed as 'ai_generated' with confidence 0.7 from GrabFood, which is a forbidden primary source; values have been re-anchored to USDA FoodData Central as the approved primary source. Confidence is set to 'medium' because the exact preparation (salt and turmeric seasoning, deep-fried kembong) required adaptation from the closest approved-source match rather than an exact entry. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat data are unavailable in the approved sources for this specific preparation.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for deep-fried kembong fish with turmeric-salt seasoning are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or HPB Singapore Foodo as of 2024. 2. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this specific deep-fried preparation is not reported in the approved sources consulted. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrient values are not fully reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this specific deep-fried preparation with turmeric-salt seasoning; HPB Foodo was consulted as a secondary cross-reference but does not provide complete micronutrient fractionation for this dish. 4. Glycemic index is estimated at 40 (low) based on analogous fried fish products in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of GI Values; no direct GI measurement exists for deep-fried kembong fish. Glycemic load per 120 g serving = 2.0 (low). 5. The input record's ethnic_tags included 'Laksa', 'Nasi Lemak', and 'Beverage' which are clearly erroneous for this food item and have been removed. 6. Potassium estimated at 350 mg/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked Indian mackerel as a plausibility cross-reference; this value is flagged as an estimate. 7. sugar_g is 0.0: confirmed as naturally zero — kembong fish contains no sugar and the turmeric-salt seasoning adds no sugar. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed as naturally zero for deep-fried fish; turmeric contributes negligible vitamin C which is destroyed by high-temperature frying. 8. Nutrient source updated from HPB Foodo as sole primary source to USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15046) as primary approved source, with HPB Foodo as secondary cross-reference, to resolve g2 approved-source compliance failure from iteration 2.
